concepts.title: The Psychological Contract: Trust as Foundation for Agile Relationships
concepts.description: The implicit agreements and mutual trust between employer and employee that enable agile working to function, distinct from formal contracts.
concepts.definition: The psychological contract is the set of implicit, unspoken agreements and expectations that govern the relationship between employer and employee—distinct from the legal employment contract. It is an agreement about how each party will treat the other: will the organization deal fairly if employees release protective constraints? Will employees self-manage if given autonomy? Agile working requires a shift in the psychological contract: from "the boss directs, employees comply" to "both sides trust each other to do what's right."
concepts.inBook:
John Heaps (Eversheds, Yorkshire Building Society) articulates this explicitly: "If agility means anything, it means the workforce are prepared to trust their organisations to deal fairly with them if they release the organisations from some of their current protection." Legal contracts are inflexible—they specify 35 hours per week, 9 to 5, etc.—because neither side trusts the other. An employee insists on exact hours to protect against exploitation; an employer insists on exact hours to ensure control. But agile working requires releasing some of these protections: people trust the organization to let them leave early if they complete their work; the organization trusts people to work late when needed, without overtime compensation, because both are committed to the mission.

This is radical in the context of employment law's history. Heaps notes: "Only 30 years ago, the textbook for employment law was Master and Servant. We've come a long way in a short time, but some deeply entrenched attitudes about management and the workforce survive that are very hard to shift. The root of the problem is a lack of mutual trust, caused by decades of bitter argument during annual negotiations about pay and conditions that have left both sides bruised and watchful."

The book offers multiple examples of trust breakdown: organizations that announce flexible working policies but secretly downgrade remote workers in performance reviews; employees who work from home but feel obligated to send emails at midnight to prove they're still working. In both cases, the psychological contract is broken—the policy is there, but the underlying trust is absent, so the policy is either ignored or exploited.

Phil Smith (Cisco) calls this the "People Deal": an explicit statement that agile working is not one-sided (employer extracts, employee accommodates) but mutually beneficial. "It's not just about what the employer expects from employees or what employees expect from their employer. It's more about the relationships between the two, and the need for a more sophisticated relationship that recognises there are sets of characteristics that each requires in the other."

Andrew Bester (Lloyds) describes the psychological contract shift needed: "The hard part now, for leaders, is to invert that and create an environment in which the right people assemble quickly to solve problems, or identify what needs to be done to fix a problem or identify opportunities, and then enable people to get on with it." This requires trusting people to self-organize, to make decisions without approval, to work unsupervised. Benedict Brogan (Lloyds) adds: "There are moments that demand great flexibility from all of us. Those moments, however, should be seen for what they are: the obvious trade-offs for the flexibility we demand and are offered as colleagues."
concepts.whyItMatters:
You cannot legislate trust. A management team can write a flexible-working policy, but if the underlying psychological contract remains one of suspicion—"we need to control people"—the policy will fail. Managers will find reasons to exclude people from remote work; employees will work excessive hours to prove commitment; the arrangement will be used as a filter to identify loyal versus disloyal employees. Trust, by contrast, is earned and modeled. When a leader works from home and completes high-quality work, employees see that presence is not required for performance. When an employee takes a personal afternoon off and then works late to finish a task, they demonstrate self-management. When both sides keep these implicit agreements, trust compounds.

This also explains why agile working hits a ceiling in many organizations. Without the psychological contract shift, agile working becomes a perk for the trusted few, not a norm. It becomes gendered (mostly women use it, damaging their career prospects) or professionalized (only individual contributors can be flexible; managers must be visible). The research shows that organizations past this ceiling explicitly invest in rebuilding the psychological contract: training managers in outcome-based leadership, celebrating diverse working patterns, measuring trust through pulse surveys, and making fairness visible through transparent promotion and pay decisions. Only when the contract shifts—from "I will control you" / "I will minimize my commitment" to "I will trust you to deliver" / "I will be fair"—does agile working scale.

concepts.title: Demand-Capacity Balance
concepts.description: The practice of explicitly visualizing and matching incoming demand against available team capacity, enabling sustainable pace and intentional demand shaping rather than reactive work acceptance.
concepts.definition: Demand-capacity balance is the explicit management of the ratio between work arriving into a system and the capacity available to complete it. Rather than accepting all incoming work and letting teams absorb the overload, this practice makes both demand and capacity visible and manageable. Scrumban enables this through WIP limits, classes of service that sort work by risk and delivery cadence, and metrics that reveal where demand exceeds capacity. Importantly, balancing includes "demand shaping"—consciously deciding which work to accept, which to defer, and which to reject—rather than attempting to expand capacity to match unlimited demand.
concepts.inBook:
Reddy argues that the Kanban Method "directly addresses the challenge of balancing demand against capability" more than any other framework, including Scrum. The book emphasizes that this goes beyond simply limiting work in progress; it includes awareness of non-value-adding demands and active reduction of waste. A concrete example: software teams often respond to requests for information about future work that may never occur, or reserve capacity to handle unpredictable, disruptive demands. Scrumban visualizes these patterns and enables teams to make deliberate trade-offs: Do we reserve 20% capacity for unplanned work, or do we require ad hoc requests to queue? Does this particular customer request actually align with our service-level expectations?

The book connects this to the Kanban Method's sustainability agenda: sustainable pace is not achieved by working harder but by limiting how much work is in flight at any moment, which also improves system agility by maintaining slack—the available options to respond to change.
concepts.whyItMatters: This concept prevents the silent dysfunction where teams say "yes" to everything and then either miss commitments or burn out achieving them. By making demand and capacity explicit and visible, teams gain leverage to have honest conversations about priorities and trade-offs. It also enables forecasting: once you know your sustainable capacity and how much demand arrives, you can predict when promises can be safely made. Organizationally, it shifts the conversation from "work harder" to "manage demand," from a moral failure model to a systems design model.

concepts.title: Explicit Policies and Visualization
concepts.description: Making all work-handling policies, constraints, decisions, and work-in-progress visible—both on the board and in written team agreements—so that nothing is implicit, ambiguous, or left to individual interpretation.
concepts.definition: Explicit policies and visualization is one of the Kanban Method's six core practices and becomes foundational in Scrumban. It means making three categories of information visible: (1) the workflow itself—what states work passes through, visualized as columns on a board; (2) the work items and their current state, displayed as cards; and (3) the policies that govern how work is handled—written agreements about when to pull new work, what "done" means, how ad hoc requests are prioritized, what happens when capacity is exceeded. The practice acknowledges that implicit policies create confusion and enable different team members to operate under different assumptions about the same work.
concepts.inBook:
Reddy details the specific policies teams should make explicit: Which individuals manage the ready buffer and replenish it? When and how? Who manages completed work and forwards it downstream? How should ad hoc requests be managed? When should work be pulled from the ready buffer? What is the definition of ready (pull criteria) and definition of done for each work type or lane? The book also addresses what not to visualize—administrative activities or 5-to-10-minute ad hoc requests may clutter the board unless teams discover they constitute significant hidden demand. Visualization policies for work items themselves include due dates, size estimates, start dates, and end dates, chosen not as a prescriptive set but as a starting point teams can evolve.

Importantly, the book distinguishes explicit policies from static processes: these policies are continuously visible and therefore continuously available for challenge and improvement. Jason Yip's GIFTS framework (Good start, Improvement focus, Focus on moving work, Team collaboration, Status communication) shows how an explicit policy for daily stand-ups shifts the meeting from status reporting to collaborative problem-solving.
concepts.whyItMatters: Explicit policies eliminate ambiguity that otherwise fragments team effort. When "done" is ambiguous, one team member calls a task complete while another assumes more work is needed; visualizing the definition of done prevents rework and debate. When ad hoc work arrival is undiscussed, it invisibly disrupts planned work; making the policy explicit (e.g., "ad hoc requests queue in a separate lane with 30% capacity reserved") lets teams see and manage the impact. This also enables evidence-based improvement: teams can discuss whether a policy is working by observing its effects on the board, not by guessing or blaming individuals. Finally, explicit policies are easier to remember, teach to new team members, and share across teams—they become part of the organization's shared language.

concepts.title: Kata as Disciplined Improvement
concepts.description: Establishing repeated, habitual practices of small-scale experimentation and problem-solving (like A3 Thinking) so that continuous improvement becomes automatic and embedded in organizational DNA rather than episodic or dependent on heroic effort.
concepts.definition: A kata (derived from Japanese martial arts) is a detailed pattern of movement or thinking that becomes habitual through repeated practice. In Scrumban, kata refers to establishing disciplined, repeated routines for continuous improvement—such as A3 Thinking (a structured problem-solving and hypothesis-testing method on a single page) or the Cynefin framework (a decision-making kata for different types of problems). The goal is to create value-adding habits that reinforce important values, so that when employees and managers seek improvements day after day, "without having to think about it, then high performance becomes sustainable."
concepts.inBook: Reddy draws on Mike Rother's concept from *Toyota Kata* that Toyota's success derives not from specific tools but from "invisible routines of thinking and acting, particularly in management, that differ significantly from those found in most companies." A3 Thinking exemplifies this: it is both a disciplined approach to problem-solving and a kata—a teaching protocol for instilling common vocabulary and thinking patterns throughout an organization. The book emphasizes that adopting a kata (or several katas) across an organization represents an ideal, but adopting some habitual discipline is a key ingredient to accelerating individual and organizational learning. For early Shu-level learners especially, katas provide the concrete steps and structure they need before abstract principles can take hold. Scrumban "naturally supports many different katas"—the framework doesn't mandate one; it enables teams to discover and institutionalize whichever works best for their context.
concepts.whyItMatters: This concept transforms continuous improvement from an aspirational goal ("we want to improve") into an executable, transmissible practice. Without kata, improvement depends on individual brilliance or episodic consulting engagements; with kata, it becomes a self-sustaining organizational capability that new team members can be trained into. It also explains why Scrumban succeeds where other frameworks stall: the framework itself is less important than the embedded thinking routines it enables. A team with deep A3 Thinking discipline will improve faster and more sustainably than a team with a perfect process design and no improvement discipline. Practically, it offers a bridge between abstract principles (systems thinking, empiricism) and concrete daily action—the kata is the mechanism that makes principles operational.

concepts.title: Layering the Kanban Method with Scrum
concepts.description: A technique for applying the Kanban Method's four principles and six practices—visualization, WIP limits, flow management, explicit policies, feedback mechanisms, and collaborative experiments—alongside Scrum's ceremonies and artifacts to unlock capabilities neither framework provides alone.
concepts.definition: Layering is the practice of deliberately weaving the Kanban Method's principles and practices into a Scrum foundation without replacing or dismantling Scrum itself. The Kanban Method contributes four principles (start with what you do now, respect current roles and responsibilities, pursue incremental evolutionary change, encourage leadership at all levels) and six practices (visualize, limit WIP, manage flow, make policies explicit, develop feedback mechanisms, improve collaboratively through experiments). Scrumban takes the position that these are wholly consistent with how Scrum was designed to operate—Scrum emphasizes local decision-making and empowers practitioners to choose how to achieve its outcomes using whatever models and tools make sense in their context.
concepts.inBook:
Reddy uses the Mammoth Bank and Objective Solutions case studies to illustrate contrasting implementations of this layering. Both organizations added kanban visualization and flow management to their Scrum contexts. Mammoth Bank, whose teams were less mature in Scrum execution, continued time-boxed iterations and used the Kanban Method's transparency to improve ceremony effectiveness and shared understanding. Objective Solutions, with mature teams experienced in Extreme Programming, used kanban's continuous-flow capability to eliminate sprint starvation—a business problem, not a team maturity issue. The book emphasizes that the Kanban Method's mechanisms for explicit policies, visualization, and feedback loops directly address weaknesses in Scrum's ceremonial structure without requiring teams to abandon ceremonies entirely.

The book notes that the Kanban Method itself had not fully matured when Ladas first coined Scrumban in 2008; today's layering is far more sophisticated, enabled by the Kanban Method's later crystallization into four clear principles and six practices.
concepts.whyItMatters: This concept unlocks the capability to improve Scrum implementations that are struggling not because of Scrum's design but because the context into which it was transplanted makes that design ineffective. Scrumban doesn't position Kanban as a replacement but as a complement that brings psychological safety, service orientation, and flow visibility—dimensions Scrum is largely silent about by design. Layering also allows an organization to preserve Scrum's benefits (team cohesion through sprints, ceremonies for coordination) while gaining Kanban's benefits (predictability, demand-capacity visibility, evolutionary change). Most crucially, this approach scales: you can layer kanban capabilities across multiple teams using the same principles without requiring a wholesale organizational restructure.

concepts.title: Pragmatic Cadence Choice
concepts.description: The decision to use either time-boxed iterations (sprints) or continuous flow—or a hybrid—based on team maturity, context, and business needs, rather than committing to one mode as doctrinally correct.
concepts.definition: Scrumban rejects the notion that time-boxed iterations (sprints) are always preferable or that continuous flow is always optimal. Instead, it enables teams to make an informed choice based on their specific circumstances: team maturity, skill stability, resource sharing constraints, and business cadence. Time-boxed iterations create synchronized commitment points and coordination rhythms that can be valuable; continuous flow enables predictability and eliminates sprint starvation for some contexts. The key principle is that this choice should be made consciously, with understanding of what each mode optimizes for and what it trades away.
concepts.inBook: Reddy critiques Corey Ladas's original Scrumban formulation—which advocated moving away from sprints toward continuous flow—as context-specific rather than universally optimal. The book argues that "factors favoring time-boxed practices include the depth of skills and maturity of your teams, the stability of the members in those teams, and the extent to which key resources must be shared." The contrasting case studies illustrate this: Objective Solutions, with mature teams coordinating across 15–50 teams with a monthly release cadence, found continuous flow ideal for eliminating sprint starvation and managing complexity. Mammoth Bank's less-experienced teams continued using time-boxed sprints because "regular interactions around the work were beneficial in helping members improve their shared understanding of both the work and the sprint goals." The book emphasizes that this pragmatic choice—either path represents an acceptable outcome—is precisely what distinguishes Scrumban from purist approaches.
concepts.whyItMatters: This concept allows organizations to move beyond theological debates about frameworks and focus instead on outcomes. It recognizes that some constraints are artifacts of context, not eternal truths: a startup with a small, co-located team of generalists may thrive with continuous flow, while a large, distributed enterprise with specialists must synchronize via sprints. It also enables evolution: a team can start with sprints (useful structure for new learners) and migrate toward continuous flow as maturity and context change. Most importantly, it frames this choice as part of systems design rather than as failure to "do Scrum right."

concepts.title: Psychological Barriers to Agile Adoption
concepts.description: Emotional and social resistance to change—fear, loss of status, disrupted identity—that Scrumban lowers by respecting current roles and building incremental evidence.
concepts.definition: Psychological barriers are the emotional and social obstacles—distinct from intellectual or technical ones—that prevent teams and organizations from adopting new ways of working. These include fear of job loss, loss of status and expertise, uncertainty about survival in a changed system, and disruption to identity (e.g., "I am a QA specialist" becomes unclear in a cross-functional team). Scrumban addresses these barriers through its Kanban Method foundation, which explicitly prioritizes psychological safety: start with what you do now (reducing the shock of change), respect current roles and titles (protecting identity), and pursue incremental, evolutionary change (building evidence before large commitment).
concepts.inBook:
Reddy argues that Kanban's first three principles are "directed at breaking down psychological barriers to learning and change" because these barriers are "often the main reasons why efforts to adopt new ways of working fail." He notes that Scrum is "largely silent about the psychological elements of learning and change" by design, which renders implementation more challenging in organizations with existing structures and identities. The book references Stephen Covey's model of moving from dependence through independence to interdependence, positioning Scrumban as a framework that nurtures interdependent relationships by emphasizing systemic understandings, building trust through consistently fulfilling promises, and leveraging intrinsic human needs (curiosity, honor, mastery, power, freedom, relatedness, status, goal, order). 

Reddy also draws on Robert Cialdini's principles of influence: Scrumban's visualization and predictability enable reciprocity; explicit commitment mechanics reinforce follow-through; infectious early successes create social proof; the scientific foundation of metrics creates authority; less contentious discussions build liking; and visible scarcity enables better-informed decisions.
concepts.whyItMatters: This concept explains why many Scrum adoptions fail despite Scrum's theoretical soundness: they ignore the psychological cost of disrupting existing roles, status, and identity. Scrumban's approach of starting incrementally and respecting current state dramatically lowers the perceived threat, which unlocks willingness to experiment. It also recognizes that trust is built through consistent delivery of promises, not through vision statements—incremental improvements that actually manifest build organizational belief in the framework. This is particularly powerful at scale: big-bang adoption efforts often trigger defensive reactions, while evolutionary improvement is often "contagious" because it demonstrates value without requiring total surrender of existing identity.

concepts.title: Scrumban as an Evolutionary Framework
concepts.description: A pragmatic approach to combining Scrum and Kanban that scales across all mastery levels (Shu-Ha-Ri), starting with what you do now and evolving incrementally rather than through disruptive big-bang adoption.
concepts.definition: Scrumban is fundamentally a framework of empowerment that supports evolutionary improvement at every stage of organizational learning. Rather than prescribing a single destination or replacing existing practices with a defined process, it provides complementary tools and capabilities that practitioners at different mastery levels (Shu: beginner following rules, Ha: intermediate discovering alternatives, Ri: advanced mastering adaptively) can deploy pragmatically based on their context. It succeeds by respecting current roles, responsibilities, and ways of working while catalyzing incremental improvements in response to discovered problems.
concepts.inBook:
Reddy introduces Scrumban not as Corey Ladas's original "transition method from Scrum to continuous flow" but as a family of principles and practices that has evolved far beyond that single incarnation. The book distinguishes Scrumban as a framework that works at all three mastery stages differently: for Shu-level learners, it provides the discipline and structure needed to introduce Scrum ceremonies and Kanban practices in limited scope; for Ha-level practitioners, it exposes new tools that help teams navigate the challenges and experiments they encounter as they discover the limits of initial practices; for Ri-level masters, it is flexible enough to provide a robust process within which they can operate at hyper-performing levels while emphasizing systems thinking and discovery.

Critically, Reddy argues this pragmatic, bottom-line orientation—achieving improved service delivery of IT work and products to customers—is precisely what distinguishes Scrumban from "purist" frameworks. When strict adherence to Scrum fails to produce results, Scrumban does not blame the organization for inadequacy; instead, it provides mechanisms to discover and resolve the actual problems present, even if the resulting process looks unlike textbook Scrum or Kanban.
concepts.whyItMatters: This concept lets teams and organizations adopt agile practices without requiring the disruptive, context-changing transformation that full Scrum adoption often demands. By starting with what exists and evolving incrementally, Scrumban reduces psychological resistance to change and allows organizations to build evidence and trust over time. Most importantly, it reframes "not doing it by the book" not as failure but as necessary adaptation—a distinction that makes the difference between sustainable improvement and abandoned initiatives. It is also the mechanism that allows a single framework to serve both teams just beginning their agile journey and mature organizations optimizing complex delivery at scale.

concepts.title: Service Orientation Lens
concepts.description: Viewing delivery and organizational structure through the lens of services delivered to customers and stakeholders, rather than as collections of functions and specializations, enabling systems thinking and cross-boundary optimization.
concepts.definition: The service orientation lens is a perspective shift that reframes how an organization sees itself. Instead of viewing delivery as a set of functional silos (engineering, QA, operations, product) or a series of hand-offs among specialists, service orientation asks: What service do we deliver to our customers? What stakeholders depend on that service? What steps and decisions must flow through our organization for that service to reach the customer? This lens enables teams to recognize that workflow is how knowledge discovery and value creation actually happen, not how specialists are arranged.
concepts.inBook: Reddy emphasizes that service orientation is one of the Kanban Method's three core agendas (alongside sustainability and survivability) and that it represents a "radical change of thinking" for most organizations. The book illustrates that seeing services delivered to customers—rather than envisioning only a collection of functions and specializations—creates "a whole new level of understanding." Service orientation deepens knowledge discovery, improves lead times, enhances predictability, and minimizes variability. Critically, the book contrasts this with the common mistake in Lean value stream mapping: following the work through a series of specialists rather than identifying the dominant knowledge discovery activity at each stage. This latter approach leads to overly complicated visualizations and institutionalizes hand-offs. The book argues that Scrumban can be scaled across an organization in a service-oriented fashion by designing each service with a systems thinking approach.
concepts.whyItMatters: This lens transforms how organizations identify bottlenecks and design improvements. Instead of optimizing for specialist utilization or process steps, service orientation drives decisions toward customer value and end-to-end predictability. It also reorients organizational sensing: a service-oriented structure naturally surfaces early when a customer's needs are shifting, because the entire value stream feels the change simultaneously rather than having it filtered through intermediary functions. This becomes a cybernetic mechanism for organizational responsiveness. Practically, it allows a single framework to coordinate across multiple teams and specializations without requiring reorganization—you overlay service orientation onto existing structure.

concepts.title: Culture Follows Structure
concepts.description: Organizational culture—how people actually behave and make decisions—is shaped by the structure and incentives in place, not by stated values or exhortation.
concepts.definition: The principle that an organization's actual culture (how people behave and decide) flows from its structure, reporting lines, and incentive systems—not from mission statements, values posters, or training. To change culture, change the structure first; changing culture while leaving structure intact is performative.
concepts.inBook: Larman identifies "Culture follows structure" as the fourth law of organizational behavior, contrasting it with a common startup belief that "structure follows culture." In established large organizations with entrenched power structures, culture is an output of structure. If you have functional specialists reporting to function managers with bonuses tied to their function's efficiency, you will get siloed, local-optimization behavior regardless of how many times leaders say "collaborate." If you want cross-functional, customer-focused behavior, you must change the reporting lines and incentive structures first. Larman notes this principle is "most applicable in large, established organizations"—startups with five people can operate by shared culture and informal structure, but once an organization grows beyond that, formal structure becomes the determinant.
concepts.whyItMatters: This principle prevents futile attempts to change culture through exhortation, training, or "mindset" work while leaving the structural incentives intact. It explains why adopting Agile practices without restructuring teams around features fails—the structure still rewards functional excellence over customer flow. It shifts diagnosis away from "the team doesn't get it" toward "the structure doesn't reward what we're asking for." For practitioners, it means: if you want different behavior, redesign the structure that produces current behavior.

concepts.title: Feature Teams
concepts.description: Cross-functional teams organized around customer value and features, not around specialized functions or components, breaking the local-optimization cycle of handoffs.
concepts.definition: Teams composed of developers, testers, analysts, and designers who collectively own the delivery of customer-visible features, end-to-end, rather than handing work between functional silos. A feature team is dedicated, cross-functional, co-located, and stable (long-lived in years, not projects), with teams themselves—not individuals—as the unit of resource allocation.
concepts.inBook: Larman distinguishes feature teams from component teams as a structural lever for eliminating local optimization. In traditional large-scale organizations, specialists group by function: all analysts together, all testers together, all developers on a component together. This creates handoffs and delays at every boundary. Feature teams dissolve these boundaries; one team spans "all functions" and "all components" needed to ship a feature. The LeSS Rule states: "The majority of the teams are customer-focused feature teams." Larman shows the transition visually: analysts, testers, architects, DBAs, and programmers move from vertical silos (each with their own manager) to horizontal cross-functional teams where a team has all the skills needed to go from analysis to delivery. This eliminates the "intermediate analyst" handing requirements to developers—now the team clarifies and builds together.
concepts.whyItMatters: Feature teams attack the root cause of delay and quality waste: handoffs, rework, and knowledge scatter. By organizing around the customer's problem rather than the organization's specialties, teams can see and optimize the whole flow, not just their piece. This structure aligns incentives toward shipping value rather than protecting functional territory, making it possible for empirical process control and lean thinking to actually take root.

concepts.title: Larman's Laws of Organizational Behavior
concepts.description: Four immutable laws describing how organizations resist change by protecting status quo structures and co-opting new initiatives.
concepts.definition: Four empirical patterns that explain why change initiatives fail in organizations. Rather than describing how change *should* work, they describe how change *actually* fails: organizations are implicitly optimized to avoid changing power structures; new terminology gets redefined to preserve the status quo; change is labeled "impractical" to deflect from real weaknesses; and culture follows structure, not the reverse.
concepts.inBook: Larman presents the four laws as a direct challenge to optimistic change management. The first law states: "Organizations are implicitly optimized to avoid changing the status quo middle- and first-level manager and specialist positions & power structures." This is not a conspiracy—it's structural. The second and third laws follow as corollaries: "any change initiative will be reduced to overloading or redefining the new terminology to mean basically the same as status quo," and any initiative "will be derided as 'purist', 'theoretical', and 'needing pragmatic customization for local concerns'—which deflects from addressing weaknesses and manager/specialist status quo." The fourth law—"Culture follows structure"—is the mechanism: changing words without changing structure leaves culture untouched. Larman applies these explicitly to fake Lean, fake Scrum, fake Kanban, and fake DevOps, showing how each gets absorbed into existing power structures rather than replacing them.
concepts.whyItMatters: These laws separate genuine transformation from performative change. They explain why adopting a new framework or terminology without restructuring fails—not due to implementation error, but by design. Understanding them makes it possible to diagnose whether a change effort is truly addressing organizational structures or merely layering new language over old incentives. This shifts energy from blaming people for "not getting it" to examining whether the structure actually rewards what the initiative claims to ask for.

concepts.title: Lean Thinking: Respect for People and Continuous Improvement
concepts.description: Lean management rests on two pillars—respecting people by developing them rather than treating them as interchangeable, and continuous improvement through direct observation and kaizen, not centralized directives.
concepts.definition: Lean thinking is an approach to sustainable product development grounded in two interdependent commitments: respect for people (developing long-term great engineers, providing mentoring and safety, building teams that evolve their own practices) and continuous improvement (through kaizen, Go See, small relentless experiments, and asking "Why?" five times to root causes rather than accepting top-down solutions).
concepts.inBook: Larman draws on Toyota's philosophy directly: "Build people, then build products" and "The root of the Toyota Way is to be dissatisfied with the status quo; you have to ask constantly, 'Why are we doing this?'" He contrasts two models: one in which "lean consultants identify problems and plan the solutions" from above (the antithesis of lean); and the Toyota model where "each individual employee is given the opportunity to find problems in his own way of working, to solve them and to make improvements." Larman emphasizes "job safety, but not role safety"—organizations keep people employed during transition but don't guarantee specific titles or functions; roles change as the system improves. The mechanics include Go See (direct observation at the Gemba, not reports), kaizen (small, relentless improvement), 5 Whys (getting to root cause), team room visualization, set-based concurrent development, and retrospectives. All of these are continuous, not episodic—they are embedded in how work happens.
concepts.whyItMatters: This principle reframes organizational improvement from a top-down, expert-driven process to a bottom-up, distributed learning system. It explains why command-and-control, even well-intentioned, underperforms—frontline people have the most detailed knowledge of problems and potential solutions. Respect for people is not softness; it is structural pragmatism: organizations that develop and trust their people to improve their own work outperform those that treat improvement as something management imposes.

concepts.title: Local Optimization vs. System Optimization
concepts.description: Organizations structured around specialties each optimize their own efficiency, creating delays and waste elsewhere; true optimization requires viewing the whole system.
concepts.definition: Local optimization occurs when each group or step in a process is run to maximize its own efficiency—analysts write perfect specs before handing to developers, developers maximize lines of code per person, testers catch defects late. The aggregate result is slow system throughput and low customer delight. System optimization subordinates local efficiency to overall customer value and cycle time.
concepts.inBook: Larman dramatizes the problem with the "watch the ball, not the players" exercise: "everyone is busy and doing their best on their task, yet the system is delivering slow and not delighting the user." He traces this through multiple structures: intermediate analysts optimize their spec clarity (local); architects optimize design purity (local); component teams optimize component efficiency (local). Each handoff between specialists adds delay, rework, and knowledge loss. The classic example is the "Contract Game"—a planning phase that optimizes forecast accuracy before development starts, creating massive front-loaded analysis and a long queue of "requirements ready to code" that create waiting and inventory. The antidote is removing local optimizations through feature teams (no analyst → developer handoff), co-location, and continuous learning. Larman explicitly asks: "what's the One True system optimizing goal?"—and answers: delivering maximum customer value per unit time (concept-to-cash cycle time, customer delight).
concepts.whyItMatters: This concept reframes efficiency entirely. A step that looks "productive" (everyone busy, high utilization) can be a major bottleneck if it creates queues for downstream steps. Understanding system optimization explains why flat utilization and slack capacity matter, why feature teams beat specialist silos, and why "be more efficient" as an instruction often makes the system worse. It is foundational to lean thinking and flow.

concepts.title: Definition of Done: Quality Commitment that Enables Transparency
concepts.description: A shared, explicit standard defining what "complete" means, ensuring Increments are truly usable and inspection is meaningful.
concepts.definition: The Definition of Done is a formal, shared standard that describes the complete state of an Increment when it meets all quality measures required for the product. It is not aspirational; work that does not meet the Definition of Done cannot be presented at a Sprint Review or released. The Definition of Done creates transparency by providing everyone—developers, product owner, stakeholders—a single, agreed baseline for what "complete" actually means.
concepts.inBook:
The 2020 guide states: "The Definition of Done is a formal description of the state of the Increment when it meets the quality measures required for the product. The moment a Product Backlog item meets the Definition of Done, an Increment is born." The guide emphasizes its transparency function: "The Definition of Done creates transparency by providing everyone a shared understanding of what work was completed as part of the Increment."

The guide also clarifies what happens when it is violated: "If a Product Backlog item does not meet the Definition of Done, it cannot be released or even presented at the Sprint Review. Instead, it returns to the Product Backlog for future consideration." It further notes that the Definition of Done may come from organizational standards (in which case all teams must follow the minimum) or be created by individual teams if no organizational standard exists. Critically: "The Developers are required to conform to the Definition of Done."
concepts.whyItMatters: Without a Definition of Done, teams and stakeholders disagree silently about what "done" means. One person thinks "code complete"; another thinks "tested in production." This hidden disagreement becomes visible only when the increment fails, and then blame flows toward whoever had the stricter standard. The Definition of Done is not bureaucracy—it is the guard rail that makes inspection honest. When a team truly holds a Definition of Done, velocity becomes meaningful (it measures real completeness, not wishful counting), retrospectives become actionable (quality problems have a named location to fix), and trust between team and stakeholders solidifies. In knowledge work, where "done" is abstract, explicit shared criteria are the only way to keep empiricism from devolving into opinion.

concepts.title: Scrum Pillars: Transparency, Inspection, and Adaptation
concepts.description: Empirical foundation for Scrum built on three pillars that enable rapid learning in complex work.
concepts.definition: Scrum is built upon empiricism—the belief that knowledge comes from experience and observed outcomes. Three pillars enable this empirical approach: transparency (making work visible), inspection (observing progress frequently), and adaptation (adjusting based on what is learned). These pillars form the backbone that turns Scrum from a checklist into a learning system.
concepts.inBook:
The 2020 Scrum Guide explicitly states that Scrum is "founded on empiricism and lean thinking." The three pillars are introduced immediately after this foundation: Transparency enables inspection, which enables adaptation. The guide gives a concrete progression: "Artifacts that have low transparency can lead to decisions that diminish value and increase risk" and "Inspection without adaptation is considered pointless."

The guide illustrates transparency through the three formal Scrum artifacts (Product Backlog with Product Goal, Sprint Backlog with Sprint Goal, Increment with Definition of Done). These artifacts are designed so "important decisions are based on the perceived state of its three formal artifacts." Inspection happens through Scrum's five formal events (Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective, and the Sprint container itself). Adaptation occurs when "people involved are empowered or self-managing" and "a Scrum Team is expected to adapt the moment it learns anything new through inspection."
concepts.whyItMatters: Most frameworks prescribe *what to do*; Scrum's three pillars define a *learning mechanism*. Teams often practice Scrum activities (run ceremonies, move cards) without activating the empirical loop. Understanding that transparency is purposeless without inspection, and inspection is pointless without adaptation, reveals what makes Scrum work—and what breaks when any pillar is weak. In any complex domain (software, product, organizational change), this pattern applies: you cannot improve what you cannot see, you cannot respond to what you do not measure, and measurement without change becomes bureaucracy.

concepts.title: Scrum Values: Commitment, Focus, Openness, Respect, Courage
concepts.description: Five cultural values that enable a Scrum Team to embody empiricism and sustain the three pillars.
concepts.definition: Successful Scrum depends on people internalizing five values: Commitment (pledging to goals and to supporting each other), Focus (prioritizing Sprint work above all else), Openness (transparent about work and challenges), Respect (recognizing each member as capable and independent), and Courage (doing the right thing and tackling hard problems). These values are not slogans but observable behaviors that sustain trust and enable the empirical pillars.
concepts.inBook:
The 2020 guide dedicates a full section to Scrum Values and anchors them directly to empiricism: "When these values are embodied by the Scrum Team and the people they work with, the empirical Scrum pillars of transparency, inspection, and adaptation come to life building trust." The guide expands on each: "The Scrum Team commits to achieving its goals and to supporting each other. Their primary focus is on the work of the Sprint. The Scrum Team and its stakeholders are open about the work and the challenges. Scrum Team members respect each other to be capable, independent people. The Scrum Team members have the courage to do the right thing, to work on tough problems."

The guide then explicitly ties values back to decisions and behavior: "The decisions that are made, the steps taken, and the way Scrum is used should reinforce these values, not diminish or undermine them." This signals that adopting Scrum's *mechanics* without its *values* is shallow—the ceremonies only work if the culture embodies them.
concepts.whyItMatters: Teams often treat Scrum ceremonies as forms to fill (show up to standup, move tickets) while ignoring the values. When a team lacks courage, hard technical problems go unspoken. When openness is missing, impediments hide. This concept names what "living" Scrum actually requires: not compliance with a process, but internalization of a value system that enables honesty, self-management, and rapid course correction. In any adaptive environment, culture—what people do when no one is watching—determines whether inspection drives real adaptation or just performative motion.

concepts.title: The Sprint as Container: Fixed-Length Iteration as the Empirical Heartbeat
concepts.description: A one-month-or-less fixed timebox containing all Scrum work, creating consistent rhythm for inspection and learning cycles.
concepts.definition: A Sprint is a fixed-length timeboxed event (one month or less) that contains all other Scrum events and work. Sprints are "the heartbeat of Scrum, where ideas are turned into value." The fixed length creates a predictable rhythm for the entire empirical cycle: planning, execution, inspection, and retrospection happen inside a known window, enabling teams to learn from experience at a consistent cadence and limit risk to what can be lost in one iteration.
concepts.inBook:
The 2020 guide emphasizes the fixed length as intentional design: "They are fixed length events of one month or less to create consistency. A new Sprint starts immediately after the conclusion of the previous Sprint." The guide explains why length matters: "When a Sprint's horizon is too long the Sprint Goal may become invalid, complexity may rise, and risk may increase. Shorter Sprints can be employed to generate more learning cycles and limit risk of cost and effort to a smaller time frame."

During a Sprint, "No changes are made that would endanger the Sprint Goal; Quality does not decrease; The Product Backlog is refined as needed; and Scope may be clarified and renegotiated with the Product Owner as more is learned." All five Scrum events (Planning, Daily Scrum, Review, Retrospective, and the Sprint itself) happen inside this container, creating concentric cycles of inspection and adaptation. The guide notes that "Sprints enable predictability by ensuring inspection and adaptation of progress toward a Product Goal at least every calendar month."
concepts.whyItMatters: The fixed length is a constraint that forces prioritization and learning. Without it, work stretches indefinitely, inspection gets deferred, and adaptation never happens. The Sprint container also enables risk management—the worst outcome is bounded to what one Sprint's worth of work can lose. This pattern (consistent-length iteration) appears across complex adaptive systems: in finance (quarterly reviews), in science (experimental cycles), in software (release windows). The Sprint makes empiricism operationally possible by guaranteeing that every team has a known moment to step back, inspect, and decide what to change next.

concepts.title: Explicit Policies: Making Work Rules Visible and Collectively Owned
concepts.description: Policies about work organization, pull criteria, WIP limits, and service classes are made explicit, visible, and changeable by consensus.
concepts.definition: In any organization, countless implicit decisions are made daily about how work is organized—when to pull new work, what "done" means, how to handle urgent items, who decides what. Kanban requires these implicit policies to be made explicit: written down, visible (ideally displayed near the Kanban board), collectively agreed upon, and openly changeable. Explicit policies include pull criteria, WIP limits, service classes, meeting times, and collaboration agreements. They are not work instructions but enablers of self-organization—they create boundaries within which teams decide how to work.
concepts.inBook:
The Official Kanban Guide dedicates a full section to explicit policies: "Every day, countless decisions are made about the organization of work, either by individuals or between groups of people. Imagine a new employee starting in your area. Ideally, she will quickly understand how work is organized through explicit policies."

The guide lists what makes a policy explicit: "Policies such as replenishing the board (when, how much, by whom), Definition of when a work activity is completed and the work item can move on ('pull criteria'), WIP Limits, Policies for handling work items of different classes of service, Meeting times and content, Other principles and collaboration agreements. All of these policies should be agreed to jointly between all parties involved including customers, stakeholders, and employees responsible for work on the board."

Critically, explicit policies are not rigid: "The policies should be placed in a clearly noticeable area, preferably right next to the board...Like all other building blocks of the system, it is necessary to check and adapt these regularly...policies are not like work instructions freeing people from the burden to make meaningful decisions. Rather, policies should enable self-organization within the group of people running a Kanban system." Effective policies are "sparse, simple, well-defined, visible, always applied, readily changeable by those providing the service."
concepts.whyItMatters: Implicit policies breed silent conflict. One person thinks "urgent means you interrupt what you're doing"; another thinks "nothing is truly urgent until planned." One thinks "done means it ships"; another thinks "done means code review." When a new decision is needed, each person invokes their own implicit model. With explicit policies, decisions are transparent—and disagreement surfaces as policy disagreement, which can be debated and changed. The guide's emphasis on making policies changeable by those doing the work (not handed down from management) means policies are living documents, not rules imposed from above. In any system where people must coordinate without constant direction, explicit policies are the difference between anarchy and self-organization.

concepts.title: Kanban as Method, Not Methodology: Management Approach Without Prescription
concepts.description: Kanban is a management method applied on top of existing processes, distinct from a methodology or framework that prescribes workflows.
concepts.definition: Kanban is intentionally not a methodology or framework. A methodology prescribes defined workflows, roles, and responsibilities specific to a domain (like software development). A framework provides incomplete scaffolding requiring customization. Kanban is neither—it is a management method or approach layered onto whatever process already exists, asking "how can we manage work better?" without saying "replace your current way of working."
concepts.inBook:
The Official Kanban Guide states clearly: "Kanban is not a methodology nor a process framework. Rather, it is a management method or approach that should be applied to an existing process or way of working. There is never a question of using Kanban versus a given methodology or framework. Rather, it is always adding Kanban using an existing methodology, framework, or way of working."

The guide elaborates on the distinction: "In software engineering a methodology is a process definition approach to software development and project management...Methodologies contain prescriptive, defined workflows and processes, including roles, and responsibilities...A process framework on the other hand is an incomplete methodology – a set of scaffolding that is intended to have broader applicability but requires customization for each context." Kanban is neither of these. Its purpose is narrower and more focused: "Kanban is intended to help you manage work better and to improve service delivery to the point where you consistently meet customer expectations. Kanban is a means to improve what and how you already do things. It is not a replacement for what you already do."
concepts.whyItMatters: Teams often treat Kanban as a replacement methodology ("we're going Kanban now") when its actual power lies in its non-prescriptive nature. A team using Waterfall, Scrum, or any custom process can apply Kanban's management principles on top without discarding their existing structure. This distinction enables adoption in complex organizations where wholesale process replacement is politically or operationally unfeasible. The freedom to "start where you are now" with Kanban makes evolutionary change possible—organizations improve incrementally without the disruption of big-bang transformation. In any established system, a method that works *with* existing culture rather than demanding it be replaced has vastly higher adoption and sustainability.

concepts.title: Kanban Principles: Change Management and Service Delivery Philosophy
concepts.description: Two foundational principle sets—Change Management (evolutionary approach) and Service Delivery (customer and workflow focus)—that guide how Kanban is applied.
concepts.definition: Kanban rests on two sets of principles: Change Management Principles (how to approach improvement) and Service Delivery Principles (how to serve customers). Change Management Principles include "Start with what you do now," "Agree to pursue improvement through evolutionary change," and "Encourage acts of leadership at all levels." Service Delivery Principles include "Understand and focus on customer needs and expectations," "Manage the work; let people self-organize around it," and "Regularly review the network of services and its policies in order to improve outcomes." Together, these principles define both *how* change happens and *what* work is for.
concepts.inBook:
The Official Kanban Guide introduces both explicitly: "Kanban encourages you to take a service-oriented approach to understanding your organization and how work flows through it. This service-oriented organizational paradigm is based on the idea that your organization is an organic entity consisting of a network of services, each of them living and breathing, and evolving."

Under Change Management: "Kanban is not a big bang transformation going from a current state to some future state...Instead, Kanban uses an evolutionary change approach, building on the way of working already in place, seeking to improve it using many forms of feedback and collaboration. The Kanban Method engenders evolutionary change through insights gained by the people working with the Kanban board and taking acts of leadership to continuously improve their way of working."

Under Service Delivery: The guide emphasizes that Kanban is "used with a service focus in mind...an effective organizational development tool." Customer focus is foundational: "We improve service delivery...improvements should be guided by a set of principles...based on the idea that your organization is an organic entity consisting of a network of services...If we are to improve service delivery, improvements should be guided by a set of principles."
concepts.whyItMatters: Most change frameworks assume a destination-driven model: define the future state, plan the transition, execute. Kanban's Change Management Principles reject this. Starting where you are and evolving incrementally works in systems too complex to forecast (most human organizations) and lowers the political cost of change because it does not require wholesale disruption. The Service Delivery Principles reframe work from "tasks to complete" to "services to customers," which reorients priorities—doing less work that matters beats doing more work that doesn't. In any organization, clarity about *how* change happens and *why* work matters determines whether improvement is sustained or merely performs for an audit.

concepts.title: STATIK: Systems Thinking Approach To Introducing Kanban
concepts.description: A six-step, iterative method for designing a Kanban system by understanding current state, demand, capability, and workflow before building the board.
concepts.definition: STATIK (Systems Thinking Approach To Introducing Kanban) is a repeatable, humane six-step iterative process for designing a Kanban system from scratch. Rather than imposing a standard board template, STATIK asks teams to first understand their current state: sources of dissatisfaction, patterns in demand, system capabilities, workflow activities, classes of service, and only then design the Kanban system (board, WIP limits, policies) appropriate to that specific context. The process is iterative—later steps may uncover new information that prompts revisiting earlier steps.
concepts.inBook:
The Official Kanban Guide presents STATIK as the answer to "If every board and Kanban system is unique, how can I design my own system?" The six steps are:

1. Identify sources of dissatisfaction – "What are the people involved in service delivery dissatisfied with? What are customers dissatisfied with?"
2. Analyze demand – "What do customers request, through which channels? What are types of work and patterns in demand?"
3. Analyze system capabilities – "What are the capabilities of the system with regard to how much of the customer demand is being delivered, of what type, and how fast and predictable?"
4. Model the workflow – "Which are the activities that each of the identified work item types go through?"
5. Identify classes of service – "How do items enter and get treated in the system?"
6. Design the Kanban system – "Based on all the insights gained in the prior steps, the Kanban system is then designed."

The guide notes: "STATIK is not intended as a one-pass, sequential process, but rather it is intended to function as a feedback loop that informs design and redesign activities. In practice, this process usually takes between 4 hours and 4 days...it should be done with at least a representative group of the people involved. While everyone will have a picture of how the work is done in mind, it rarely maps between people. The STATIK approach will reconcile these views into a shared view."
concepts.whyItMatters: Most teams copy a standard Kanban template without understanding their actual workflow, demand patterns, or pain points. STATIK inverts this: understand *before* designing. This ensures the board reflects reality, not wishful thinking, and that the design choices (WIP limits, workflow columns, service classes) solve actual problems. The iterative nature acknowledges that system design is not one-time; as teams learn, the system evolves. In any complex domain, designing without understanding the system you are designing for guarantees a solution that does not fit. STATIK embodies the Kanban principle "Start where you are now" by making diagnosis a non-negotiable first step.

concepts.title: EBM Learning Loop: Continuous Improvement Through Measurement and Experimentation
concepts.description: A five-step cycle (quantify, measure, select, experiment, evaluate) that translates value measurement into organizational improvement.
concepts.definition: The EBM Learning Loop is a five-step cyclical process for improving organizational value delivery: (1) Quantify Value—define Key Value Measures for the Key Value Areas; (2) Measure KVMs—establish baseline measurements; (3) Select KVAs to improve—prioritize which areas to target; (4) Conduct experiments—test practices hypothesized to improve the selected KVAs; (5) Evaluate results—compare post-experiment measurements to baseline, and iterate. This loop runs continuously, making small changes, measuring impact, and cycling again rather than waiting for large improvements or annual strategic reviews.
concepts.inBook:
The Evidence-Based Management Guide presents the loop as answer to the question "How to Improve Empirically using EBM": 

"1. Quantify Value – The first step in the EBM learning loop is to quantify value in the form of KVMs. The process of defining and aligning around KVMs itself might be valuable to an organization, because it can create transparency around what is being optimized."

"2. Measure KVMs – The next step...is to establish initial values or a baseline measure for the KVMs of interest. This step provides an initial view of the viability of the product and the ability of the organization to deliver the product. It creates transparency around relative strengths and weaknesses."

"3. Select KVAs to improve – With a clear view of current organizational value...managers...can now make informed decisions about which KVAs would be most valuable to change. Don't try to affect too many KVAs within a single learning loop. Making small incremental changes and then quickly measuring the result is better than delaying improvement and measurement by changing too many factors at once."

"4. Conduct practice experiments – After choosing a desired KVA to improve, select, at most, a few practices...and run an experiment."

"5. Evaluate results – Once the results of the experiment are measured, they should be compared with the KVM values before the changes were made. If the experiment produced improved measured, the change is kept, otherwise other improvements are tried. The learning loop continues until the KVA reflect the desired results."

The guide emphasizes iteration as the core mechanism: "Improving organizational performance is also a cyclic, iterative process: measuring current conditions, setting performance goals, forming small improvement experiments that can be quickly run, and then measuring again to gauge the effect, then repeating, continuously."
concepts.whyItMatters: Most organizations improve sporadically: a crisis drives change, or an annual strategy review identifies fixes. The EBM Learning Loop makes improvement continuous and empirical. By running many small experiments with rapid feedback, teams avoid the trap of large changes that produce unmeasurable impact. The loop also forces clarity about *what* to improve (it surfaces disagreements when teams choose which KVA to target). In any complex adaptive system, continuous small iterations with measurement beat episodic large changes with uncertain impact. The loop's power lies in its discipline: you only proceed to experiment if you've measured baseline, and you only keep changes if you've measured impact.

concepts.title: Four Key Value Areas: Holistic Measurement of Organizational Capability
concepts.description: Four dimensions (Current Value, Time-to-Market, Ability to Innovate, Unrealized Value) that together measure both what a product delivers and the organization's ability to sustain delivery.
concepts.definition: Evidence-Based Management (EBM) organizes value measurement into four Key Value Areas (KVAs): Current Value (CV) measures what the product delivers to customers today; Time-to-Market (T2M) measures the organization's speed to deliver new capabilities; Ability to Innovate (A2I) measures capacity for significant new features; and Unrealized Value (UV) measures potential future value. Organizations without strength in all four may deliver short-term wins but cannot sustain them. EBM requires measurement across all four to understand both current performance and long-term viability.
concepts.inBook:
The Evidence-Based Management Guide introduces the four KVAs as an integrated framework: "Each KVA focuses on a different aspect of either value, or the ability of the organization to deliver value. Organizations without strength in all four KVAs may deliver short-term value, but will not be able to sustain it."

Current Value asks: "How happy are users and customers today? Is their happiness improving or declining?" and "How happy are your employees?" and "How happy are your investors?"

Time-to-Market asks: "How fast can the organization learn from new experiments? How fast can you learn from new information and adapt? How fast can you deliver new value to customers?"

Ability to Innovate asks: "What prevents the organization from delivering new value? What prevents customers or users from benefiting from that innovation?" Examples include "spending too much time fixing defects, having to maintain multiple code branches, a complex application architecture, insufficient product-like environments to test on, lack of operational excellence, poor code management practices, lack of decentralized decision-making."

Unrealized Value asks: "Can any additional value be created for our organization in this market or other markets? Is it worth the effort and risk to pursue these untapped opportunities? Should further investments be made?"

The guide emphasizes integration: "Delivering value, happy stakeholders, and satisfied employees (Current Value) are important, but organizations must also show that they can meet market demand with timely delivery (Time-to-Market) while being able to sustain innovation over time (Ability to Innovate). Continued investment in the product is justified based on measures of as-yet (Unrealized Value)."
concepts.whyItMatters: Teams typically measure what is easiest: output metrics (velocity, features shipped). EBM forces measurement of outcomes and capacity. A team shipping fast (good T2M) but delivering features no one uses (poor CV and UV) is succeeding at the wrong thing. A team with high CV but declining A2I is harvesting a mature product, not building the future. The four-area framework prevents the common trap of optimizing one metric (speed) while destroying another (innovation capacity). In any organization, how it measures success determines what it optimizes for. The four KVAs ensure measurement reflects both today's results and tomorrow's viability.

concepts.title: Leading and Lagging Indicators: Different Types of Measurement for Different Decisions
concepts.description: Leading indicators detect changes rapidly, enabling fast response; lagging indicators show impact after delay. Mixing them causes decisions to trail reality.
concepts.definition: A leading indicator detects a change in Key Value Measures (KVMs) with relative speed, enabling faster response. A lagging indicator only shows change after a long delay. Critically, the same measure can be either leading or lagging depending on measurement frequency—revenue measured daily is a leading indicator; revenue measured only quarterly is lagging. Choosing which indicators to track determines whether an organization responds to problems early (leading) or discovers them post-hoc (lagging). True leading indicators allow inference about likely outcomes of lagging indicators before the lagging indicator has time to move.
concepts.inBook:
The Evidence-Based Management Guide states: "Leading indicators detect changes in KVMs with relative rapidity, enabling faster response, while lagging indicators may only show changes after a long delay. Many indicators are neither intrinsically leading or lagging, but only become one or the other depending on how frequently they are measured. Thus, when revenue is measured every day, it is a leading indicator, but when it can only be measured monthly or less frequently it becomes a lagging indicator."

The guide provides concrete examples: "Build and integration success is a fair predictor for the likely stability and predictability of the overall release" (leading), while "Revenue per Employee, or the Product Ratio...are influenced by so many contributing factors that they provide mostly only general ideas of how well the product is creating value" (lagging).

For customer satisfaction: "Leading indicators of customer satisfaction are hard to obtain, but usage data can serve as a proxy. Transaction abandon rates can give insight into successful completion of an activity, and simple usage data can at least inform whether features are being used." For employee satisfaction: "Leading information about Employee Satisfaction can be gathered using something as simple as a 'How was work today?' button."

Importantly, the guide warns about causality: "It requires human judgment and bottom-up intelligence to have meaningful data-driven conversation to hypothesize about the root causes behind shifts in indicators. These conversations might trigger new actionable insights, hypotheses and experiments."
concepts.whyItMatters: Organizations that track only lagging indicators (quarterly revenue, annual customer satisfaction surveys) are always fighting the last battle—problems are visible only after they have compounded. Leading indicators (daily build success, weekly usage patterns, real-time support ticket volume) enable early course correction. However, leading indicators are harder to identify and require more frequent measurement. The distinction forces teams to choose: do we want to know about problems early (and pay for frequent measurement), or do we accept latency in our knowledge? In any adaptive system, lag between decision and feedback determines response speed. The faster the feedback, the tighter the learning loop.

concepts.title: Definition of Done at Scale
concepts.description: A shared standard defining what "done" means, with policies and checklists, that ensures transparency across distributed teams and evolves with maturity.
concepts.definition: A Definition of Done is a shared agreement within a Scrum team about what "done" means for a work item—ensuring that everyone understands when work is truly complete. It consists of policies (how to test and validate) and checklists (required tasks reflecting organizational standards), and it varies by team context and maturity level.
concepts.inBook:
The Scrum Guide requires that when a Product Backlog item is described as "Done," everyone must understand what that means. The book shows this takes two forms: validation policies (e.g., "all defects found when testing a story must be fixed immediately," "acceptance tests must be satisfied") and task checklists (e.g., "code is checked in to repository," "build is successful," "code has been reviewed," "unit tests developed"). 

Critically, the Definition of Done is not static. Teams with little Scrum experience start with relatively simple definitions, then as they gain maturity and add automation, they evolve toward more sophisticated definitions that include Definition of Ready. The book also shows that Definition of Done varies among teams—an operations team may have a different DoD than an innovation team—but having *common definitions at scale* helps portfolio leaders and others understand what "shippable" means. Sprint-level DoD asks "what is potentially shippable?" while Release-level DoD asks "what does it mean to release your product?"
concepts.whyItMatters: Distributed teams naturally drift toward different understandings of completion—one team's "done" might be another's "in progress." An explicit, agreed Definition of Done converts this silent miscommunication into a single point of control. It surfaces quality expectations upfront, prevents rework, and makes it possible for portfolio leaders to reason about whether work is truly shippable. As a team matures and automates more, the Definition of Done becomes a mirror of their technical discipline.

concepts.title: Definition of Ready
concepts.description: An agreement on prerequisites work must satisfy before the team starts, ensuring requirements are clear, risks identified, and impediments resolved.
concepts.definition: A Definition of Ready is an agreement on what conditions must be met before a work item enters a sprint—a "ready-to-start" gate that mirrors Definition of Done's "ready-to-release" gate. It ensures that requirements are understood, dependencies surfaced, impediments removed, and work is sized by the team that will do it.
concepts.inBook:
The webinar positions Definition of Ready as addressing a maturity phase: teams that have mastered Definition of Done realize that "Done" is about ending well, while "Ready" is about starting well. The book shows examples including: requirements understood, test plan imaginable, no impediments, sized by team doing work, high priority on backlog, and conversations have happened to create acceptance criteria.

The book contrasts this with traditional requirements documents, which abstract away critical details—it shows a humorous requirement example ("the product shall have four wheels") that misses what really matters. At Conteneo, Definition of Ready included user story maps, data models, problem briefs, lo-fi sketches, and hi-fi interaction models—whatever the team agreed they needed before starting.
concepts.whyItMatters: A Definition of Ready prevents teams from starting work that will derail mid-sprint due to unclear requirements, missing information, or hidden dependencies. By establishing what "ready" means at a distributed scale, teams reduce rework, false starts, and context-switching costs. Ready gates also protect teams from being asked to estimate poorly-defined work or commit to items that have impediments the team cannot remove.

concepts.title: Shippable Quality Standards
concepts.description: A framework using severity and impact dimensions to prioritize production bugs into fix-immediately, negotiate-release, or backlog categories based on customer risk.
concepts.definition: Shippable Quality Standards use a two-dimensional severity/impact matrix to classify defects and decide whether they must be fixed before release, can be negotiated, or belong in the backlog. Severity measures the effect on the customer (data loss, crash, error), while impact measures how many customers are affected. The intersection determines priority action.
concepts.inBook:
The book shows that preventing production bugs requires building quality criteria into Definition of Done rather than trying to sort them later. It provides a concrete severity/impact matrix where severity ranges from "Data Loss" (extreme) to "Feature Request" (not a bug), and impact ranges from "80%+ of customers" (I4) to "1-10% in special conditions" (I1).

The combined matrix categorizes responses: Data Loss/Many Customers = "Fix Immediately"; Crash-No-Workaround/Most Customers = "Fix in Sprint"; Crash-with-Workaround/Some Customers = "Negotiate Release"; lower severity/impact combinations = "Add to Backlog (Maybe)" or "Delete." The book emphasizes that this framework prevents the ad-hoc prioritization debates that consume time and that quality standards should be part of Definition of Done to stop bugs from reaching production in the first place.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without explicit standards, organizations waste cycles debating whether each bug must be fixed now. A severity/impact matrix makes the decision rule explicit, transparent, and repeatable across teams. It also prevents two pathologies: over-fixing low-impact issues and under-prioritizing high-impact ones. By embedding quality standards in Definition of Done, teams catch problems earlier rather than waiting for production failures.

concepts.title: Adaptive Governance
concepts.description: Collaborative, flexible, learning-based management of programs and portfolios that empowers teams through principles and boundaries rather than controlling through detailed plans.
concepts.definition: Adaptive Governance is the collaborative, flexible, and learning-based management of programs and portfolios at scale. It replaces traditional plan-driven governance—which emphasizes control through detailed plans, process standardization, and upward reporting—with a model that clarifies principles and boundaries, then trusts teams to make decisions within them.
concepts.inBook:
The document positions Adaptive Governance as the evolution needed to overcome barriers to sustained Scrum adoption at scale. While agile methods like Scrum have enabled project-level evolution from plan-driven to adaptive management, organizations must now evolve from plan-driven governance to adaptive governance at the portfolio level.

Traditional PMOs are perceived as "process police" that block Scrum's success—they lack authority, focus on upwards reporting, enforce rigid process standardization, and suffer from staff-versus-line organizational structures that create overhead. Adaptive Governance flips this: Agile PMOs consider Scrum teams to be their customers and support them through project prioritization and selection, portfolio tracking using agile techniques, stable resource management, and sustained adoption support. The organization oscillates between centralized information discovery (where one role gathers and reports back) and network-based integration (where richly connected nodes hear everyone's opinion and make collaborative decisions).
concepts.whyItMatters: Organizations scaling Scrum beyond single teams face a control dilemma: traditional governance models constrain the very flexibility that makes Scrum work, while no governance at all produces chaos and misaligned priority battles. Adaptive Governance offers a third path—clarifying the business constraints and values, setting portfolio limits (WIP, cycle time), and trusting teams to optimize within those boundaries. This preserves local autonomy while maintaining portfolio coherence.

concepts.title: Portfolio Prioritization by Business Value
concepts.description: Using Little's Law and business metrics to rightsize project portfolio, limit work-in-process, and re-prioritize regularly by value for optimal flow.
concepts.definition: Portfolio Prioritization by Business Value addresses the typical portfolio pathology: too much work-in-process, no prioritization by business value (instead driven by politics), resource over-utilization, and dangerous variation. It uses Little's Law (Cycle Time = WIP / Completion Rate) and business value metrics to right-size the portfolio, limit concurrent projects, and maintain regular re-prioritization.
concepts.inBook:
The document shows that typical project portfolios suffer from: too many in-flight projects, no business-value-based prioritization, resource over-utilization, and large batch sizes with irregular service rates. Adaptive Portfolio Realignment addresses this through: terminating sick projects, splitting large projects into smaller ones, prioritizing projects by business value (at least within business units), limiting development timeframe to months, and re-prioritizing regularly.

The underlying science is Little's Law: Cycle Time = WIP / Completion Rate. When WIP is too high, cycle time explodes.
